segments.s522-g65: This is a poetic, inverted noun phrase where the predicate adjective 'پر' (full) and its complement 'از آرزو' come before the noun: دل پر از آرزو = 'heart full of desire'. The second noun phrase is parallel: لبان پر ز باد = 'lips full of wind/breeze'; here ز is the poetic variant of از marking 'filled with'. Both 'پر' are adjectival 'full (of)', not the noun 'feather'.
segments.s522-g66: همی داشت ... به یاد is a periphrastic construction: همی (continuous aspect marker) + داشت (past auxiliary used to form past-continuous) + verb phrase 'گفتار ایشان به یاد' = 'kept their words in memory'. 'ایشان' is the third‑person plural possessive/subject ('their'/'they'), and 'به یاد داشتن' = 'to remember/keep in mind'. Word order places the object phrase after the auxiliary.
segments.s522-g67: چهارم (the fourth) precedes the verb بشد (became/happened) — a common narrative order in classical Persian. بشد here is past/narrative 'became/there came', and مرد روشن‌‌روان is the predicate noun + adjective 'a bright‑souled man' describing who appeared.
segments.s522-g68: That clause begins with the relative/complementizer که 'who/that'. بگشاید is the classical present/subjunctive form of گشودن used here as a narrative/future‑oriented verb 'would open/reveal' or simply 'revealed'. آن راز is the direct object 'that secret', and با + proper name (با اردوان) marks the person with whom/for whom the secret is disclosed (here 'to/for Ardavan').
segments.s522-g69: برفتند is simple past 'they went/left' and appears first (verb‑initial narrative). با زیجها is an instrumental/ accompanimental phrase 'with the zijes (astronomical tables)'. برکنار functions adverbially 'aside/away' indicating they moved off to the side or departed from there.
segments.s522-g70: This is a compound prepositional phrase: ز کاخ کنیزک = 'from the palace of the maidservant' (ز = poetic از 'from'; here کاخِ کنیزک with the ezāfe relation expressed without an explicit -e) and بر شهریار = 'toward/to the king' (بر used in the sense 'toward/to'). Together they indicate they left her palace and proceeded to the king's presence.
segments.s522-g71: بگفتند is the past 'they said/spoke (of)'. The object phrase راز سپهر بلند = 'the secret of the lofty heavens' follows the verb; سپهر (heavens/sky) is modified postpositively by the adjective بلند (lofty), a typical poetic order.
segments.s522-g72: همان حکم او = 'that very decree of his' (همان emphasizing identity; او = 'his'). بر here introduces the scope or topic ('concerning/about'), so بر چه و چون و چند lists the interrogative categories treated: 'چه' = what, 'چون' = how, and 'چند' = how many/how much. The string therefore means 'his decree concerning what, how, and how many'.
segments.s522-g73: کزین پس is a fused poetic prepositional phrase (modern از این پس) meaning 'from this time on/thereafter'. کنون is an adverb 'now' placed for emphasis. تانه (poetic only/alone) restricts بس روزگار: بس روزگار is a noun phrase 'sufficient / a span of time' (بس = 'enough', روزگار = 'time/term') with the adjective-like بس preceding the noun as is common in older Persian word order.
segments.s522-g74: ز چیزی is a prepositional phrase 'from/by anything'; بپیچد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive form with the prefixed ب- (classical/polite/optative) meaning 'be turned / be swayed'; دل نامدار is the logical subject 'the famed heart' placed after the verb for poetic emphasis. Together the clause says that the famed heart should not be turned/swayed by anything.
segments.s522-g75: که here functions as a subordinate conjunction 'so that/that'. بگریزد is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive 'may/might flee, escape' (prefixed ب-). از introduces the object of fleeing; مهتری کهتری is a paired antithesis 'the higher and the lower' (preeminence vs. inferiority/high vs. low), a succinct poetic way to say 'from both the greater and the lesser'.
segments.s522-g76: سپهبد is a military title 'spahbed / general'. نژادی here functions adjectivally (literally 'of descent/by lineage') and in context names or characterizes the spahbed; و is the simple conjunction 'and'; کنداوری is presented as a proper name/place (a lexicalized name in the manuscript). The whole phrase lists two named persons/titles.
segments.s522-g77: وزان (و + زآن) = 'and from that' / 'thereafter'; پس reinforces temporal sequence 'then/after that'. شود is the 3rd-person singular present/past-in-story 'become/arise'. شهریاری is 'sovereignty/kingship' and بلند is an epithet 'lofty/loftily placed' following the noun as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s522-g78: جهاندار is a compound title 'world-holder' i.e. sovereign/ruler. The sequence و نیک‌اختر و سودمند strings epithets/names: نیک‌اختر here functions as an epithet or proper name (the text treats it like a title/name), و is 'and', سودمند is an adjective meaning 'useful/beneficent'. Together they enumerate titles/qualifying names of the resulting rulership/person.
segments.s522-g79: دل نامور is 'the famed/renowned heart'. The following مهتر نیک‌بخت is a nominal apposition: مهتر 'the chief/elder/distinguished man' named نیک‌بخت (Nīk-bakht, 'fortunate one'). In Persian poetry possessive/attributive relations and titles often sit side-by-side without a linking particle, so this reads as 'the famed heart of the chief Nīk-bakht' or 'the famed heart — the chief Nīk-bakht'.
segments.s522-g80: ز گفتار ایشان = 'from/because of their speech/words' (ز = from/because of, ایشان = they/them). غمی is the direct object 'a grief' and گشت is the verb 'became/turned into' (past/resultive in narrative). سخت is an adverb/adjective 'severe/very' modifying غمی گشت — 'became a severe grief'.
segments.s522-g9: چنان بد که is a fixed poetic way to introduce a circumstance ('so/thus it was that'). روزی برآمد is an idiomatic simple-past clause: روزی ('one day') + برآمد ('arose/went up') and به بام ('onto the roof') marks the destination. Word order is topic/circumstance (چنان بد که) → time (روزی) → verb (برآمد) → goal (به بام).
segments.s522-g10: دلش گشت is 'his heart became' (دل + enclitic ـش + گشت 'became'); زان 'from that' links the result to the preceding action and خرمی شادکام is a compound describing the resulting state ('delightful, content'). The adjective/nominal phrase خرمی شادکام functions as predicate complement of گشت.
segments.s522-g11: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction where نگه ('gaze') plus کرد ('did/made') yields 'he gazed' or 'he looked'. خندان‌لب is a compound adjective 'smiling-lipped' modifying اردشیر (the subject follows in this word order), so the phrase reads 'Ardashir, with smiling lips, looked.'
segments.s522-g12: جوان here denotes 'the youth/young man' (subject); در دل ماه شد جایگیر is a verbless‑like clause where شد جایگیر ('became settled') is the verb phrase and در دل ماه ('in the heart/of the moon' — a poetic simile) gives the locus or simile. Overall: 'the young man became settled (as) in the heart like the moon.'
segments.s522-g13: همی بود is an imperfect/continuous past sense ('he was still / it continued to be'); تا + clause ('until') introduces the time limit: روز تاریک شد ('the day became dark'). Word order: continuous-state marker → copula → temporal boundary → result clause.
segments.s522-g14: همانا is an emphatic particle ('verily/truly'). به شب روز نزدیک شد is a locative/toward construction: به شب ('toward night') modifies روز نزدیک شد ('the day came near' / 'the day approached the night'). شد is the simple past/copular 'became' used here with نزدیک.
segments.s522-g15: کمندی بران کنگره بر ببست is a sequence of short actions: کمندی ('a lasso') + بران ('on/over that') + کنگره ('battlement') + بر ('on') + ببست ('he fastened/secured'). The verb ببست comes last and governs the preceding object (he fastened the lasso on the battlement).
segments.s522-g16: گره زد is the idiom 'he tied a knot' (گره 'knot' + زد as light verb 'made/struck' to mean 'tied'); برو چند is an instruction-like fragment 'let it go / give it (a little)' where برو is imperative/verb 'go' and چند indicates a small amount; و ببسود دست is a blessing/wish: ببسود ('may it profit / be of use') + دست ('hand'). The sequence describes tying and testing the rope and invoking that the hand be useful/blessed.
segments.s522-g41: The verb گرفتند (they took) appears at the start by poetic inversion; here it combines with یاد to form the idiom «یاد گرفتن/گرفتن یاد» meaning 'to remember' or 'to take into one's mind'. هر مهتری is the plural/collective subject 'every chief' placed after the verb for rhythm, and پارس is the object 'Pars/ Persia' (what is remembered).
segments.s522-g42: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed'. The preposition به plus مهتر پسر ('to the chief's son') marks the indirect object; داد is the past verb 'gave' and پارس (placed last, for emphasis) is the direct object 'Pars'. The word order (indirect object before verb, direct object after) is a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s522-g43: بفرمود is an honorific past 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces a subordinate clause with a purposive/subjunctive sense ('so that' / 'that they should'), so کوس بیرون برند is subordinate and برند is the subjunctive/plural verb 'they bring/carry out' (بردن). The phrase means 'he ordered that they bring the drum out.'
segments.s522-g44: This continues the command: ز درگاه لشکر ('the army from the court' — ز marks origin/place) and به هامون برند ('bring/lead to the plain' — به + destination). Again برند is the subjunctive verb echoing the prior clause; the prepositional phrases show movement from the royal court toward the plain.
segments.s522-g45: جهان تیره شد is a copular/resultative phrase 'the world became dark'; بر دل اردشیر marks the experiencer ('upon Ardashir's heart' — Persian uses بر دل کسی to indicate what affects someone emotionally). Thus the clause says Ardashir was cast into gloom (literally: the world darkened on his heart).
segments.s522-g46: ازان ('from/ because of that') gives the cause; پیر روشن‌دل و دستگیر is an appositive descriptive phrase 'that old man, clear-/bright‑hearted and helpful.' روشن‌دل and دستگیر are adjectival epithets modifying پیر, the whole phrase explaining why Ardashir's heart darkened.
segments.s522-g47: دل از لشکر اردوان برگرفت is an idiomatic past action: برگرفت ('took back / removed') with دل 'heart' and از لشکر اردوان 'from Ardavan's army' means he withdrew his affection/support from Ardavan's forces. The subject (he) is implied from the narrative context.
segments.s522-g48: وزان ('and from that/thereupon') links the result: آگهی 'news/information' is the trigger, and رای دیگر گرفتن literally 'to take another counsel/plan' means to change one's policy. گرفت is the past verb 'he took/adopted' and here means 'he resolved upon / adopted' another course of action.
segments.s522-g33: بیایم is a first‑person optative/subjunctive form meaning 'may I come / I will come' often used with conditionals. چو here is the conditional 'if'. خواهی is second‑person present meaning 'you wish'. به + نزدیک + تو is a prepositional phrase 'to/near you' with به (to/towards) followed by نزدیک (near) and the post‑nominal pronoun تو (you).
segments.s522-g34: درفشان here functions adjectivally/participially ('shining, brilliant') rather than the alternate noun 'standard‑bearer'. کنم is the first‑person present/subjunctive of کردن 'to do/make' (I will cause/make). روز تاریک تو is the object phrase 'your dark day' with possessive pronoun تو placed after the noun (typical Persian word order).
segments.s522-g35: چو is temporal 'when'. لختی is an adverbial noun 'a short while'. برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/came up'. برین is written here as بر + ین = بر این 'upon/over this' (بر = on/upon; ین is the clitic/orthographic form of این 'this'). روزگار = 'time/era'.
segments.s522-g36: شکست is the noun 'defeat'. اندر آمد is an idiomatic past construction 'came into' = 'befell' (اندر = in/within; آمد = came). به آموزگار is a prepositional phrase ('upon/onto the teacher') marking who is affected.
segments.s522-g37: Two epithets precede the name: جهاندیده 'worldly‑wise' and بیدار 'awake/alert'. بابک is the proper name. بمرد is an older/classical past form meaning 'he died' (here functioning as the simple past of مردن).
segments.s522-g38: سرای کهن دیگری is the direct object phrase 'the old/ancient house to another' (سرای = house, کهن = old/ancient, دیگری = another). را is the definite/accusative marker attaching to the object. سپرد is the past transitive verb 'he entrusted/handed over'. Note: the provided sense list for سرای did not include the noun 'house', so that sense is marked -1.
segments.s522-g39: چو is temporal 'when'. آگاهی here means 'news/information' (a noun) and آمد is 'came' (past). سوی اردوان is a directional phrase 'to Ardavan' using سوی = toward/to and the proper name اردوان.
segments.s522-g40: پر از غم شد is a standard copular/constitutive phrase 'became full of sorrow' (پر = full (of); از = of; غم = grief; شد = became). و = 'and' links the clauses. تیره گشتش روان = 'his spirit/psyche (روان) grew dark' where گشتش = گشت + ش (گشت = turned/became; ش = enclitic 'his/it') and تیره = dark/obscure.
segments.s522-g1: This is an archaic word order: the predicate 'یکی کاخ بود' (there was a palace) comes before the possessor 'اردوان را'. 'یکی' = 'a certain' introduces existence; 'کاخ بود' = 'there was a palace' (بود is past 'was'); 'اردوان را' marks the possessor (classical Persian uses را with the possessor in constructions like this) and 'بلند' is the adjective 'lofty' modifying 'کاخ'.
segments.s522-g2: This string describes location and a resident: 'به کاخ اندرون' = 'within/into the palace's inner part' (به functioning as a locative/towards), followed by 'بنده‌ای ارجمند' = 'an esteemed servant' (بنده‌ای, indefinite with -ای; ارجمند modifies it). Word order is locative phrase then the noun phrase describing who was there.
segments.s522-g3: 'که' introduces a relative clause ('whose ...'), 'گلنار' is the personal/name token, 'بد نام' is a compound adjective here attached to 'نام' (classical usage can carry the sense 'called/known as' or 'ill‑named' depending on context), and 'آن ماه‌روی' is a further epithet: 'that moon‑faced (beautiful) one'. Word order places epithets after the name in a poetic, compact form.
segments.s522-g4: 'نگاری' here is a poetic noun for a beloved/beautiful one or a pictured beauty; 'پر از' is the idiom 'full of' (پر followed by the preposition از), and the list 'گوهر و رنگ و بوی' enumerates qualities (gems, color/complexion, and scent). The phrase describes the woman's adornment and allure.
segments.s522-g5: Here 'بر اردوان' means 'with respect to Ardavan' or 'for Ardavan', 'همچو' = 'such' (demonstrative), 'دستور' = 'order/arrangement/custom', and 'بود' is the past copula 'was' — together: 'Such an arrangement was for Ardavan' (i.e. Ardavan had such a practice/arrangement).
segments.s522-g6: 'بران' = 'for that / with respect to that' (connecting back to the previous clause), 'خواسته' = 'desired' (here indicating 'as he wished'), 'نیز' = 'also', 'گنجور' = 'treasure‑laden' or 'treasury', and 'بود' = 'was'. The whole segment means the treasury was likewise as he desired — i.e. his wishes extended to the treasury as well.
segments.s522-g7: 'بروبر' is a classical adverbial/prepositional expression meaning roughly 'before/face‑to‑face' or 'toward', here introducing the manner of the action; 'گرامی‌تر' is the comparative 'more honored/more dear'; 'از جان' = 'than (his) life' (از used for the comparative 'than'); 'بدی' in this poetic past form is a finite past copula/verb indicating 'he/you was/were' in older Persian — here it functions as the past verb of the clause. Note: the printed lemma senses for بدی in the data do not match this verbal use, so treat it as the past copula in context.
segments.s522-g8: 'به دیدار او' = 'at meeting him' or 'on seeing him' (به + noun for temporal/locative sense), 'شاد و خندان' are a coordinated descriptive pair 'happy and smiling', and the final 'بدی' is again the archaic past copula 'was' (indicating his state at the meeting). The clause describes his cheerful appearance upon meeting the other.
segments.s522-g17: به گستاخی is an adverbial phrase of manner: به + noun (گستاخی, 'audacity') meaning 'with audacity' or 'boldly'. از باره آمد فرود is a poetic ordering of a verb phrase: آمد (past 'came') + فرود ('alighted/descended'), with از باره ('from the place/occasion') giving source; note the verb follows the phrase (typical Persian word order can be varied in poetry).
segments.s522-g18: همی داد is a continuous/habitual past construction: همی + past verb داد gives 'kept giving' / 'was giving'. نیکی modifies درود (a kindly/good greeting). دهش را is the definite object marked by را (here presented as the recipient of the greeting); درود is the noun 'greeting' — overall: 'kept giving a kind greeting to X.'
segments.s522-g19: بیامد (past 'came') is the main verb; خرامان is an adverb/adjectival participle meaning 'with a proud/stately gait' and modifies the verb. بر + اردشیر marks direction/target: 'toward Ardashir' (بر here = 'to/toward'). The phrase order places manner and direction before/around the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s522-g20: پر از is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'full of' and takes a following list of nouns joined by و. گوهر, بوی, مشک, عبیر are listed as the things he is 'full of' (jewels and scents). In Persian the 'full of' construction uses از after پر (پر از X).
segments.s522-g21: ز بالین دیبا is a prepositional phrase 'from the brocade pillow' (ز = 'from', بالین = 'pillow', دیبا = 'brocade/silk cloth'). سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ـش 'his'), and برگرفت is the past verb 'raised/took up'. The verb follows the prepositional phrase and the clitic marks possession on سر.
segments.s522-g22: چو بیدار شد is a temporal subordinate 'when he became awake' (چو = 'when', بیدار = 'awake', شد = past auxiliary 'became/was'). تنگ در بر گرفت is an idiomatic verbal cluster: تنگ 'tight/close' + در بر گرفتن ('to take into one's arms / to draw (garment) close around oneself') where گرفت is the past verb 'took'. The phrase describes drawing something tightly about the body.
segments.s522-g23: نگه کرد is a simple past 'he looked/gazed' (نگه as noun/verb-stem 'gaze' + کرد 'did' is common in classical Persian). برنا بران خوب‌روی strings adjectives/demonstratives: برنا 'youthful', بران (poetic demonstrative/locative) 'on/that', خوب‌روی 'good-faced/fair-looking'. The order is poetic: verb + adjectives/demonstrative marking the person gazed upon.
segments.s522-g24: بدان is a compound demonstrative 'to/at that' (here split as ب + دان in the apparatus); it introduces the list موی و آن روی و آن رنگ و بوی — 'that hair and that face and that color (complexion) and scent'. The repeated آن ('that') before روی and رنگ is emphatic/parallel. This segment is a coordinated noun phrase listing striking features the speaker inspects.
segments.s522-g25: بدان (= به آن) is an older/poetic form meaning "to that" and introduces the indirect object (to that moon). ماه is the addressee "the moon." گفت is the past tense verb "said"; the quoted question follows: از کجا (from where) + خاستی, where خاستی is an archaic/past 2nd-person singular of خاستن meaning "you rose/appeared." Word order places the recipient (بدان ماه) before the reporting verb گفت, then the quoted interrogative phrase.
segments.s522-g26: که introduces the subordinate clause ("that / who"). پرغم is an adjective/compound meaning "full of grief" modifying دل (heart); the possessive enclitic ـم on دل marks "my heart." را is the definite/object marker attached to the object phrase. بیاراستی is an older past form (2nd-person) of آراستن/بیاراستن meaning "you adorned/arranged"; here it states the moon has beautified the speaker's sorrowful heart.
segments.s522-g27: چنین is an adverb meaning "thus/so." داد is the past tense "gave" and پاسـخ is the noun "answer," together "gave such an answer" or "answered thus." که introduces the reported content. من بنده‌ام is a self‑humbling statement: من (I) + بنده with the 1st‑person enclitic ـام giving "I am a servant/servant I am," a common idiom of humility.
segments.s522-g28: ز (poetic 'from' / 'of') + گیتی (the world) sets the domain/existence "in the world." به دیدار تو is a purposive phrase: به + دیدار (a noun meaning "meeting/sight") + تو (you) → "for the seeing of you / to meet you." زنده‌ام is the stative/predicative form with 1st‑person suffix ـام: "I am alive." The phrase means essentially "I live in the world to see you" or "I live for the sake of seeing you."
segments.s522-g29: This is a nominal string of names/epithets. دلارام is a female proper name; گنجور is an epithet literally "treasure‑bearing/treasury‑holder" (here forming a title or epithet attached to the name); شاه اردوان names the king (Ardavan). The sequence places the proper name and epithets together as one NP ("Dolaram, the treasure‑holder of King Ardavan" or similar).
segments.s522-g30: که introduces a relative clause: "who/which." از من بود uses از + pronoun to mean "on account of me / because of me" and بود (past of بودن) states existence/condition "was." شاد و روشن‌روان are predicative adjectives joined by و: "happy and bright‑souled/cheerful." Together: "who, because of me, was happy and light‑souled."
segments.s522-g31: کنون‌گر is an archaic/poetic conditional phrase meaning "if now" (گر = if). پذیری is the 2nd‑person present/subjunctive of پذیرفتن/پذیرفتن "you accept/receive" (classical form). Here ترا is split as the enclitic object/oblique pronoun (ت + را) marking the addressee/object; the clause ends with بنده‌ام (I am a servant), the humbling predication with 1st‑person suffix ـام. The whole segment expresses the conditional: "If you accept (me) now, I am your servant."
segments.s522-g32: دل و جان is a paired noun phrase "heart and life/soul." به مهر تو is a prepositional phrase meaning "with/by your love" (مهر = love/affection, تو = your). آگنده‌ام is a predicative adjective/participle (آگنده = "filled with") with 1st‑person suffix ـام: "I am filled (with it)." The clause states cause/condition: "my heart and life are filled with your love."
segments.s522-g49: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/because'); از here means 'because of' (causal). درد او = 'his pain' (درد is emotional sorrow). بد دلش is a descriptive phrase: بد + دل + ش ('ill/discontented heart, his heart') with the possessive suffix ش. پرستیز is a poetic noun placed after دلش to characterize that heart ('disdain of pomp/reputation' or scorn for honor) — together the phrase says his heart, ill at ease because of his pain and averse to pomp.
segments.s522-g50: به هر سو = 'toward every side/in every direction' (به + هر + سو). همی is the archaic/poetic iterative particle ('kept/continually'); جست is the classical-form verb meaning 'leapt/sprung' (here: moved urgently); راه گریز = 'a way of escape' (راه modified by گریز). Word order is adverbial (به هر سو) + verb (همی جست) + object/goal (راه گریز).
segments.s522-g51: ازان پس = 'from that time on / thereafter'. چنان بد که is a correlative construction meaning 'so (bad) that...' — چنان (so) + بد (bad/ill) + که (that) introduces the result clause. شاه اردوان is the subject/agent that the result clause will describe (the clause continues in the next segment).
segments.s522-g52: ز here is the poetic form of از meaning 'from/among'; اخترشناسان is a plural noun 'astrologers/astronomers' and روشن‌روان is an epithet placed after it ('bright‑souled' or 'clear-minded') qualifying those astrologers. Grammatically this is a noun phrase ('from among the bright‑souled astrologers') that will serve as the object/source in the following clause.
segments.s522-g53: بیاورد is a classical narrative form meaning 'he brought' (3sg past/brief past in literary register). چندی here is an adverb 'for a while'. به درگاه خویش = 'to his court/royal presence' — درگاه is 'court/threshold' and خویش is formed from the root خوی plus the possessive suffix ش (= 'his own'). The whole segment is a verb + temporal + prepositional phrase: he brought (them) for a time to his court.
segments.s522-g54: همی is the iterative particle 'continually/again'; بازجست is an old/narrative past of 'search/examine' (they examined/sought). اختر و راه خویش = 'the star and his road/path' (اختر = star, راه = course/way, خویش = his own). The verb precedes its objects in this classical order: they examined the stars and his (future) course.
segments.s522-g55: همان نیز = 'that likewise/that as well' (referring back to the previous action). تا here is the temporal conjunction 'until'; گردش روزگار is an idiomatic nominal phrase 'the turning/cycle of time' meaning the course of fate or the passage of time — گردش is 'turning/circuit' and روزگار is 'time/fate'. The segment sets a temporal limit: 'until the course of fate...'.
segments.s522-g56: ازان پس = 'from that time on / thereafter'. کرا is an archaic/interrogative or indefinite pronoun meaning 'who' or 'whoever' (used in older Persian). باشد is the classical subjunctive/present form of بودن used here to mean 'will be / may be' (optative/future sense). آموزگار is the predicate 'teacher' or 'instructor' — together 'who thereafter shall be the teacher.'
segments.s522-g57: فرستادشان is a past-tense verb with the enclitic object -شان ('them'): 'he sent them'. نزد is a directional/prepositional word meaning 'to/in the presence of' and governs the following name; گلنار شاه is the addressee (name + title) placed after نزد.
segments.s522-g58: بدان here functions as a contracted direction/purpose ('to that one' / 'to him' — classical be + ān) leading into a purpose clause. تا + کنند introduces a purposive subjunctive ('so that they may do / so that they might'), with کنند as the present-subjunctive form of کردن. اختران را is the definite object ('the stars') marked by را, and نگاه is the noun 'a look / gaze' used with کردن (i.e. 'to look at'), so the clause means 'so that they may look at/observe the stars.'
segments.s522-g59: This is a duration-and-location expression: سه روز is the duration 'three days'; اندر is a classical preposition 'in/within'; آن کار 'that affair' is the locative/topic; شد is the past verb 'happened/passed.' The final روزگار here names the course of time (placed after the clause for poetic emphasis).
segments.s522-g60: نگه کرده شد is a periphrastic passive/resultive: نگه (watch/keeping) + کرده (past participle) + شد (auxiliary 'was'), giving 'was kept under watch / watch was kept.' طالع شهریار is a genitive construction where طالع ('fortune/ascendant') is qualified by شهریار ('the king'), so 'the king's fortune/horoscope.'
segments.s522-g61: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' گنجور is the subject (here a name or epithet); بشنید is the simple past 'he heard.' آوازشان is the object 'their voice(s)/their voices,' with the possessive -شان.
segments.s522-g62: This is the content of what was heard: سخن گفتن is an infinitival verbal noun 'talking/speaking'; از governs the topic 'about/concerning'; طالع 'fortune/horoscope' and و رازشان 'and their secret(s)' form the coordinated object of سخن گفتن (رازشان = راز + possessive -شان). In context the whole phrase means 'their talk about the fortune and their secrets.'
segments.s522-g63: This gives the temporal frame: (سِی)م روز / روز (day) + تا 'until' + شب 'night' + گذشته 'having passed' — together meaning 'from day until night had passed.' The final سه پاس is a parenthetic time-count 'three watches' (three guard-shifts / watches) specifying the interval. Word order reflects poetic spacing.
segments.s522-g64: کنیزک is a diminutive 'little maid/young maidservant.' بپردخت (classical past) here indicates an action completed with respect to her — in context it functions like 'was sent / was dealt with / was put into action' (the exact nuance depends on the verb's classical usage). ز is the poetical preposition 'by/from' and اخترشناس is 'the astrologer' (the agent/source). Together, the phrase says that the little maid was handed/sent by the astrologer.

sentences.s522-s33: His heart was full of longing, his lips filled with the breeze; he kept their words in mind.
sentences.s522-s34: A fourth, a bright‑souled man, came who would disclose that secret to Ardavan.
sentences.s522-s35: They departed with the astronomical tables and, leaving the maidservant's palace, went to the king.
sentences.s522-s36: They related the secret of the high heavens — that very decree of his about what, how, and how many.
sentences.s522-s37: From this time forth, may the famed heart not be turned by anything, not even for a short span of days.
sentences.s522-s38: so that he might flee from high and low ranks — the spahbed Nezhadi and Kandauri.
sentences.s522-s39: And thereafter there arose a lofty sovereignty — a world-ruler, Nīk-akhṭar, and beneficent.
sentences.s522-s40: The famed heart of the chief Nīk-bakht became a grievous sorrow because of their words.
sentences.s522-s5: So it happened that one day he climbed onto the roof; his heart, from that delight, became joyful and content.
sentences.s522-s6: Ardashir, smiling-lipped, looked; the young man became settled in the heart like the moon.
sentences.s522-s7: He stayed on until the day darkened; truly, the day drew near to the night.
sentences.s522-s8: He threw a lasso over that battlement and fastened it; he tied a knot, gave it a little pull, and may the hand be of use.
sentences.s522-s21: Every chief remembered Pars; the spahbed gave Pars to the chief's son.
sentences.s522-s22: He commanded that they bring the war-drum out and lead the army from the court to the plain.
sentences.s522-s23: The world grew dark on Ardashir's heart because of that old man, bright‑hearted and helpful.
sentences.s522-s24: He withdrew his heart from Ardavan's army, and from that news he adopted a different plan.
sentences.s522-s17: If you wish, I will come near you and make your dark day shine.
sentences.s522-s18: When a short while came in that time, defeat befell the teacher.
sentences.s522-s19: The worldly‑wise, vigilant Babak died and entrusted the old house to another.
sentences.s522-s20: When the news reached Ardavan, he became full of sorrow and his spirit darkened.
sentences.s522-s1: Ardavan had a lofty palace; in the inner apartments of the palace there was an esteemed servant.
sentences.s522-s2: whose name was Gulnār, that moon‑faced beauty — a beloved adorned with jewels, color, and scent.
sentences.s522-s3: Ardavan had such an arrangement (or rule); for that he also had a treasury as he desired.
sentences.s522-s4: He honored him more highly than (his) life; at meeting him he was joyful and smiling.
sentences.s522-s9: He descended again with audacity, and repeatedly offered a courteous greeting to Dehesh.
sentences.s522-s10: He came, striding, to Ardashir, full of jewels and the scent of musk and ambergris.
sentences.s522-s11: He raised his head from the brocaded pillow; when he woke, he drew his robe tightly about him.
sentences.s522-s12: He gazed at that youthful, fair-faced one — at that hair, that face, that color and scent.
sentences.s522-s13: He said to that moon, "From where did you rise that you have adorned my sorrowful heart?"
sentences.s522-s14: She/it answered thus: "I am a servant; I live in the world to behold you."
sentences.s522-s15: Dolaram, the treasure‑lord (Ganjur), of King Ardavan — who because of me was joyful and light‑souled.
sentences.s522-s16: If you accept (me) now, I am your servant; my heart and life are filled with your love.
sentences.s522-s25: Because of his pain his ill heart, disdainful of honor, leapt in every direction seeking a way of escape.
sentences.s522-s26: After that he was so ill/bad that King Ardavan...
sentences.s522-s27: He brought them for a while to his court and they examined the stars and his course.
sentences.s522-s28: Let that remain so as well until the turning of fate shows who thereafter shall be the teacher.
sentences.s522-s29: He sent them to Golnār the king so that they might observe the stars.
sentences.s522-s30: Three days passed in that affair; time kept watch and the king's fortune was observed.
sentences.s522-s31: When Ganjur heard their voices, he heard them talking about the fortune and their secret.
sentences.s522-s32: From day until night had passed — three watches — a young maid was (sent/)dealt with by the astrologer.
